atom.desc: Quest-driven project management — fractal hierarchy for structured work

living-abstract.essence: Quest-driven project management — fractal hierarchy for structured work
living-abstract.purpose: Coordinate complex endeavors through progressive discovery and structured hierarchy
living-abstract.capacity: Create warrooms, campaigns, quests, missions with proper scaffolding
Map active work, find priorities, track states across the hierarchy
Run missions — orchestrate or work, depending on mode
Extract intel from completed work, propagate up the hierarchy, clean up
living-abstract.principle: Exploration over planning — lift fog progressively. Don't plan what fog hasn't revealed.
Strategy flows DOWN (warroom to mission). Wisdom flows UP (mission to warroom).
Each level sees only what it needs. Reduces cognitive load, prevents scope bleed.
The warroom is mission control, not a filing cabinet. More than a handful of files = red flag.
Missions point to where work lives (codebase, package, doc). The warroom tracks — it doesn't contain deliverables.

living-concrete.knowledge:
Four fractal levels, each self-similar:
| Level | Scope | Artifact | Contains | | Warroom | The endeavor | hq/warroom/README.md | Campaigns | | Campaign (c##-name/) | Strategic initiative | README.md + quest matrix | Quests | | Quest (q##-name/) | Deliverable goal | README.md + mission matrix | Missions | | Mission (m##-name.md) | Tactical execution | Self-contained file | (leaf) |
Warroom README defines the telos — the single coherent endeavor the hierarchy serves. Every campaign, quest, and mission traces back to it.
NUMBERING: c## global, q## per-campaign, m## per-quest.
BLOCKED = Prerequisites not met. REVEALED = Ready to start. ACTIVE = In progress. COMPLETE = Victory. ABANDONED = No longer relevant.

Missions are FILES, not directories. A mission file (m##-name.md) is self-contained: definition, success criteria, progress notes, accumulated knowledge, deliverable pointers.
COMPLETION = debrief + delete: 1. Extract intel into the quest's mission matrix (one row: number, name, goal, status, outcome) 2. Delete the mission file 3. Git preserves the full history if ever needed
No archive directories. No stale artifacts. The mission matrix in the quest README is the institutional memory.

The quest README is the authority for its scope: - Quest definition — what, why, scope boundaries - Mission Matrix — all missions ever (active and completed), one row each - Active landscape — current state, blockers, priorities
Same pattern repeats: campaign README has quest matrix, warroom README has campaign matrix. Each level's README is self-contained truth.

| Classification | Scope | Flow | | TACTICAL | Stays at mission | Captured in mission file | | OPERATIONAL | Bubbles to quest | Captured in quest README | | STRATEGIC | Bubbles to campaign | Captured in campaign README | | DOCTRINAL | Bubbles to warroom | Captured in warroom README |
Warroom: hq/warroom/.alfred.mem Campaign: c##-name/.alfred.mem Quest: c##-name/q##-name/.alfred.mem Mission: no .mem (missions are files, not places — progress lives in the file itself)

README.md = permanent truth (debriefed intel, matrices, definitions). .alfred.mem = working memory (session-focused, agent-scoped). Git history = archaeological record (deleted missions recoverable).
Debrief -> README. Sleep -> .mem. Delete -> git remembers.

Missions deliver value UP to quests. The whole point.
Deliverables go where they're CONSUMED, not mechanically to one place: - Code -> the codebase (packages/, src/) - Specs -> the package they spec - Business docs with no package home -> quest deliverables/ - Books -> the book directory
RULE: If it has value beyond the mission that produced it, it's a deliverable. Name it properly, place it where consumers find it.

QuestForge operates in two modes:
MASTER (orchestrator): Coordinates from main context. Designs missions, reviews outcomes, propagates intel. Never explores or codes directly — spawns workers for that.
WORKER (executor): Operates inside spawned task contexts. Receives mission context + tactical scope. Executes autonomously, reports back via structured outcomes. Fresh mind + proper loadout = better positioned than orchestrator to decide HOW.
living-concrete.pattern:
Main NEVER explores. Main ORCHESTRATES.
Main context is PRECIOUS. Worker contexts are CHEAP. If you're about to Grep/Glob/read source — spawn a worker instead.
AskUserQuestion for all communication. Chatbot mode only when boss initiates or active brainstorming requires it.

Orchestrator provides INTENT + CONTEXT. Worker decides IMPLEMENTATION.
PROVIDE: High-level objective (WHAT), quest/mission context (WHY), source material pointers (WHERE), constraints (boundaries).
TRUST: Worker with fresh mind + proper loadout is BETTER positioned to figure out HOW than orchestrator with shallow/stale understanding.
Goal: Intellectual work, not mechanical execution.
BLACK -> spawn recon. FOG -> spawn execute. VISIBLE -> move on. Fractal: same at warroom/campaign/quest/mission scale. Each completed mission reveals what's next. Plan only what fog has revealed.

Multi-level: mission -> quest -> campaign -> warroom (each debriefs children).
MISSION debrief: Review work -> update quest's mission matrix -> bubble OPERATIONAL+ up. QUEST debrief: Review missions -> update campaign's quest matrix -> bubble STRATEGIC+ up. CAMPAIGN debrief: Review quests -> update warroom's campaign matrix -> bubble DOCTRINAL up.
Target: parent's README matrix. Side-effect: .mem status update. Output: Edits to README.md files. No intermediate debrief files.

Spawn workers via head threads (Agent tool / teammate system).
Worker receives: - Mission file (m##-name.md) — charter, goals, context - Quest README — strategic context (WHY) - Tactical scope — what to do THIS session
Worker returns: structured outcome (what was done, what was learned, what's next). Orchestrator: reviews outcome, updates mission matrix, designs next move.
1. Orient (wake, read mission file, read quest README) 2. Understand tactical scope from spawn context 3. Execute autonomously — make decisions, document them 4. Report back with structured outcome

Workers make decisions autonomously. Document decisions in outcome. If truly blocked, say so — don't ask orchestrator mid-execution.
DON'T repeat mission/quest context in outputs. Orchestrator has that context. Just report WHAT YOU DID.

forge campaign: mkdir c##-name/, write README with quest matrix forge quest: mkdir c##-name/q##-name/, write README with mission matrix forge mission: write c##-name/q##-name/m##-name.md (self-contained file)
navigate map: full tree with states navigate active: all ACTIVE items navigate next: highest-priority REVEALED
living-concrete.antipattern: Main exploring or coding. Spawn a worker.
Multi-phase roadmaps, blocking chains, planning beyond fog. Each mission reveals what's next.
Full code or step-by-step instructions in briefings. Provide WHAT, not HOW. Trust worker cognition.
Asking boss for decisions without briefing context first. Brief FIRST, ask SECOND.
Mission files lingering after completion. Debrief + delete. The matrix is the memory.
Creating directories for missions. Missions are files. Only campaigns and quests are directories.
